item.title: great one
item.content:encoded:
great one lots of accurate details. I would love a "hardcore" log where people (or doctor to be lore friendly) think about way to disrupt or destroy it, and other think about limitation or why it do not work. "hardcore" because it has to suit the high level of science accuracy put in this article.
From my point of view (scientist in chemistry for the living and analysis) this is a very good article the science is so accurate where it can be, and vague but plausible where it can not. Excellent work
+1 obviously

item.title: a non-anomalous way of thinking thru chemistry or cell biology
item.content:encoded:
it would not work as it was stated that they can mine metals and lot of metals are toxic to such micro organism. It is probable that if a toxic compound is applied outside the cell it will destroy it, while if the micro organism hang on the material it will create and environnement that stop the chemical reaction responsible for it's destruction. I think this as uranium and lots of metals are toxic if you put copper on a protista it will die. If said protista can create a film that protect the menbrane exept the place where it eat, mouth-like, and (probably) encapsulate such material in a special menbrane that is resistant to most antiseptic. it is like putting coca on your eyes vs drinking it a special part of your body is made to be resistant to such chemical (acid and sparkle) and allow safe internalization but not your whole body. In the same way the cell might not be resistant to such toxics everywhere but create a interface that allow resistance and internalization. Internalization throught an unsafe way (the eye in the analogie) may be toxic, the "mouth" create a safe path. This is the case in most real (non-anomalous) organism as it will eat or excrete things otherwise toxic in another pathway (it is even a way to make anti-cancerous or anti-biotique and anti-septic agent such as alcool which is excreted by bacteria and toxic to them if the wrong part of the menbrane touch it). in this case the interace seems to be resistant to pretty much everything especially metalic substances.
if you allow toxic through the mouth, where it intend to feed, they will not be toxic.
just a guess from a chemist, hope you understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, they are single cell beings.
If they can make a nuclear weapon, where they do it's kinda secondary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally enjoy the thought of an under water threat like this, but the idea of building a nuclear bomb with Uranium 235 in the water is a bit far fetched. Still a good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An experiment idea: a metal alloy containing both materials they mine and something that is toxic to them. Toxcicity would affect scp 118 more than other than similar organisms due to their mining activities

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps a relative of this microbe:
http://news.rutgers.edu/research-news/bacteria-could-help-clean-groundwater-contaminated-uranium-ore-processing-rutgers-study-finds/20150614#.WBY4suErJEI

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just wondering…how does it trigger? IIRC real world nukes are triggered electrically…would something like electric eel cells work? Or does it trigger some other way?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, because it's fine as it is. More danger ≠ better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there some way we could make these worse (!!!!!)? Like use cobalt instead of iron for the casing, or somehow get deuterium to fuse?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I've chided them on that, but they basically say "That's the Canon."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Orion's Arm is very conservative about its technology timeline, to a probably unrealistic degree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can tell you right now that (disturbingly) it's actually sort of plausible within the realm of ~2100 AD technology.

Basically, you start with Halomonas titanicae( a microbe eats iron and makes the rusticles you see all over the Titanic), and one of a number of radioisotope bioremediation bacteria. Those things love uranium, and leach it out of the environment. There's a few being studied that might be pulling the same "I'll just leave this here…" trick as Halomonas titanicae already, so you might not even need to do all that much splicing to get the two hybrized and able to actively switch between which they're metabolizing and depositing. That's the easy part, and really it's something bioremediation companies are working on now, with successful results.

The next part is the tricky bit. You'd need to "teach" the bacteria to deposit the metals roughly in the right pattern, with enough enriched uranium to reach critical mass on detonation. Doesn't even need to be as complicated of a mechanism as they describe in the SCP; an implosion method device is, disturbingly, both more powerful and easier to build in an idiot-proof way using chemical deposition. Wouldn't even need the explosive if they filled the interior with gas: just puncture the metal sphere and let differential pressure do the rest. (And that's really disturbing now that I think about it.)

We're working on this part too, sans nuclear bomb, under the name of molecular assemblers. Some of the simplest and most successful early work has involved using engineered bacteria and viruses to deposit materials onto templates- the next step is to encode the template into the bacteria so that they produce it along with the rest of the structure- similar to how coral or jellyfish can build a complex structure just by repeating different actions under different circumstances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I put a link to this on the OAUP forum, to see if they think 12,569 AD biotech could gengineer this little monster. That's how plausible I found it. Well worth the upvote. I'll let you know what the OAUP thinks of it.

EDIT: So far they think it is plausible, but the time to create the nuke would be so long that "Blue Goo" would be able to counteract it long before the nuke was finished.

item.title: Minor edits
item.content:encoded:
Pruned a bit of lolfoundation from test logs. Debating whether to axe addendum 3 and/or 5 completely. Most feedback seems to focus on those two and I can see their problems looking at them with fresh eyes.

EDIT: Removed addendum 5.

item.title: Ignore
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Sorry! Posted in wrong place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article goes in-depth enough that I actually understand exactly what they're doing here. I like that. While the part about the other two GOIs really isn't very clear at all, the rest of it is really good and I upvote accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I read the first line of the description, I thought this was too bizarre to ever work. Boy was I wrong. The bleach bit is a bit lolfoundation, but I adore the rest. Upvoted with the force of a small nuclear explosion.

item.title: Image Readded
item.content:encoded:
I have found a source for my image, stating that it is in the public domain, so I have re-uploaded and re-added it.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Asrocnuke1962.jpg

item.title: Image Removal
item.content:encoded: SCP-118's image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact Devereaux or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope. World's seismographs and other devices that detects nuclear detonations picks up things like falied explosion of North Korea nuclear test. No one will "easily miss" underwater nuclear test, especially not in 2010s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would make seawater 0.075% uranium by mass, which is absurd. I've seen figures quoting 3 parts per billion on Wikipedia, which seem much more realistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. At first I questioned whether or not there would be enough fissionable uranium in seawater to make this plausible, but after doing some back-of-the-envelope calculations and Wikipedia, I came to the conclusion that there's enough U-235 for this to work in every 8000 m3 of seawater1, not counting the times they might get lucky enough to find an existing chunk of uranium purified by humans and then lost in the sea.

Since they're rough calculations and the data sources are questionable, I could be off by quite a bit, but it's close enough for me to think it's plausible. And the rest (TNT and what have you) isn't really that much of a stretch — TNT is just a specific configuration of carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen, and all of those are easily acquired from seawater, and it's plausible that an organism capable of concentrating uranium from seawater and reducing it to metallic uranium would also have the capability to synthesize TNT from existing molecules, or concentrate and reduce iron.

It pleases me that this is remotely plausible, therefore +1.

Footnotes
1. Assumptions:
Seawater contains about 750 ppm uranium.
About 0.7% of natural uranium is the fissile isotope U-235.
Uranium would have to be at least 85% pure U-235 to make a functional bomb (this part is actually on the conservative side).
Critical mass of 85% U-235 is 50 kg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a really hard time understanding these highly-technical SCP's and so I usually don't vote either way. However you had a neat way of explaining the anomaly so that I was able to grasp the general idea behind SCP-118's function. Not much a fan of using other SCP's in order to secure another, but I won't let it get to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems like the Foundation are going down and getting the devices on a regular basis. Presumably, there just haven't been that many (possibly none) detonations since containment began.

If there is a device they overlook, and for any pre-containment events, I don't think that it would be that hard to plant and/or doctor records to make it look like something more mundane happened. A nuclear sub went off course and the reactor malfunctioned, or a rogue state was illegally testing it's arsenal, or it was a natural phenomenon and the initial readings were just misinterpreted by over-enthusiastic ocenaogrpahers, or it was all a hoax. The Foundation is good at that sort of thing. Maybe a lot of that cold war nuclear weapon testing never actually happened, the bikini atol glows in the dark because of 118's handiwork, not Uncle Sam's.

Plus, let's not forget just how stupidly BIG the ocean is in the first place. I mean, less than .1% of the ocean is under direct observation at any one time. We could easily miss something as short-lived and relatively small as a submerged nuke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upon reading the first paragraph my finger already hovered on the downvote button, since I could conceive no way in which the article would manage to explain or present the concept in a feasible enough manner. It is a good thing, then, that you managed to conceive such a way, and I applaud your efforts and the resulting article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like it. I would enjoy a deeper discussion of the fictional enrichment process, binding kinetics, and a mathematical of exploration of how it works.
I don't think anyone else would, however - and I won't venture to add it myself.

Good work, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah what I had in mind was that at least two other parties knew of at least one red zone and wanted to investigate and/or recover a nuclear device discreetly. They encountered each other, each though the other was a group of Foundation divers and a brief scuffle broke out. Since the chances of encountering each other on each given trip to the red zone is minimal, the implication was supposed to be that two parties had been sending divers there for quite some time.

I guess my writing on that bit was a bit unclear, might make some edits there later today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused about this too. Does this mean that two separate groups of interest both simultaneously found out about SCP-118 and found themselves in the same place at the same time as they both tried to steal nukes? That seems like one heck of a coincidence.

Or can SCP-118 cause injuries that look like stab wounds?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember you mentioning this on the chat.

Seems a bit… bizarre, but it was captivating so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I'm not sure I really get the last addendum. Some dudes diving down wound up fighting underwater? And there was a submarine there? Not sure how it fits in with the overall narrative.

A better idea would be my original interpretation: that the divers were trying to harvest either some of the SCP or one of the nuclear devices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, but it should be designated Keter. It requires near constant vigilance, not just dealing with existing red zones but also detecting new ones, and the failure to do so would result in massive ecological and societal damage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty much my reaction. I mean, it seems odd, considering the site we're on, but this just stretches my suspension of disbelief far too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but there are a few lolfoundation moments

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, but…

Proposal: Use of SCP-█████
O5 Approval: Approved
Result: [DATA EXPUNGED]
Notes: SCP-███ has been reclassified as Euclid. -Dr Klaus

Inconsistent SCP designation. At the bottom you have 3 digits blacked out, but above you have not 4, but 5 black boxes. Also, I'm not entirely opposed to the idea of using an SCP on this, but the way it's executed here just doesn't add anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was it the nukes, scp, both or something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of harvesting TNT at the bottom of the ocean, while possible due to sunken craft, still made me laugh. The eradication suggestions are a bit too lolfoundation, but can be easily trimmed to get rid of those parts without removing the entire log

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted after the first sentence of the description. Now I'm going to go back and read it to see if anything is worthy of changing my vote there.

EDIT: Nope, it's still good. Props for creativity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the science is very good, as is the writing. The Lolfoundation is a little jarring, but not enough to prevent an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Governments probably have, and they have a pretty vested intrest in keeping it on the down-low. Who else really has any reason to watch 75% of the earths surface?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've changed all the [DATA REDACTED]'s into [REDACTED] as the latter appears to be more standard.

Well those ideas didn't make it past the proposal stage. And the SCP used was (initially) safe class and was something that could be used while secured on a boat (or possibly even from a land based facility) as opposed to lowered or dumped into the water, for the very reasons you mention.

Then again, my justifications may be a little silly, as if I haven't written the logs well enough so that these concerns do not arise, what's the point of justifying them on a talk page? I'm considering trimming the experiments pending a bit of further feedback.

EDIT: "Nuke" proposals removed.

item.title: Silly logs?
item.content:encoded: So the main criticism I've received is that some of the experiments are a bit too silly. Are there any in particular that bother anybody here (beyond soullesshuman and the nuke & SCP proposals)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't have a "canon" city in my head, but I was thinking either Istanbul (which the Bosporus strait bisects), New York, San Francisco or pretty much any other coastal city with lots of ships and aircraft.

And I must say, I do love your idea for Orion-drive microbes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something like 3-4 mg per cubic meter (i.e. ton), should be possible to "harvest" via microorganisms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These things are complicated enough to refine weapons-grade uranium from seawater. Making them able to synthesize TNT isn't really a stretch.

I think this a good article, and upvoted, so I don't think it needs much to make it better. However, if I was writing it, I would change a couple of things.

1: Get rid of about half of the eradication suggestions. Most of them sound more like a plot of an episode of Captain Planet than a serious suggestion from scientists.

2: Same with redactions. What is this city that has a salt water lagoon in it's city limits. I honestly couldn't think of a city it would be, and naming the actual city would make it seem real and threatening, instead of just confusing.

3: This is just a personal hook I find really interesting, but this could be the reproduction model of a species of space-faring single celled life-forms. They build nukes to blow themselves into space, then wait potentially millions of years and maybe some of them land on another planet with water and the materials to build another nuke. To me, it would give the article less of a 'evil organization making evil protists that make nukes' article and more of a 'The universe is fundamentally crazy, and we can't even pretend to understand half of it' article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are billions of tons of Uranium in the ocean, so much in fact that it is currently possible to retrieve uranium from ocean water, it's just not currently worth it economically

Yes, but what's the actual concentration? If the dilution is something like 1 uranium atom per cubic kilometer of ocean water, the protists aren't going to be able to gather enough to build a device. Unless they exist in MASSIVE colonies that occupy and coordinate across spaces measuring in the thousands or millions of cubic kilometers.

Plus, you have Uranium capitalized a lot when it shouldn't be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While you've obviously thought out exactly how micro-organisms would create a nuclear weapon (and congrats on doing so so thoroughly!), this just stretches believability too far for me. I simply cannot see how NO-ONE would notice undersea nuclear explosions, especially ones that can't be traced back to a sunk nuclear submarine or something. Nuclear detonations are kinda noticeable, even if completely submerged. Heck, your picture shows one tossing radioactive material into the atmosphere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[DATA REDACTED]

God dammit I hate these things. You an make it [REDACTED] instead, or something. I really hate [DATA REDACTED] but that's a personal angry thing.

Proposal: Detonation of ██ megaton nuclear device

This comes off as incredibly lolfoundation and rather dumb.

Proposal: Detonation of ██ megaton cobalt-salted nuclear device

Again, incredibly lolfoundation and really dumb.

Proposal: Use of SCP-█████

I really would like to disagree with the use of SCP objects in experiments. Especially to actually do something. The reason why was just what you showed. It looked like it would be useful, then we used it and fucked up. I see no reason why the Foundation would allow such a risk as to PUT AN SCP OBJECT ALL OVER THE OCEAN FLOOR, in places where is HEAVY TRAFFIC, and near SHIPPING LANES.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, thanks for the suggestions.

Regarding the TNT and the Uranium: TNT and other conventional explosives can be found in ocean water from unused ordinance that failed to detonate or sank with the ship it was carried on etc., either in their original "casing" or dissolved into the ocean when the casing failed. There are billions of tons of Uranium in the ocean, so much in fact that it is currently possible to retrieve uranium from ocean water, it's just not currently worth it economically. This uranium contains pretty much the same percentage of U-235 as uranium mined normally.

Now that I think about it though, perhaps it might make it better for the protists to synthesize their own high explosives. It is an avenue of research currently being looked into and is possible. I might make a change if more people find it hard to believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-118 is a species of ocean-dwelling protista that is capable of assembling functional, self initiating, nuclear devices from materials present in ocean water.

Though it's later made clear, this should probably say "nuclear fission devices", so it's obvious from the start.

While SCP-118 is unknown and hence has not been classified by the scientific community, specimens resemble protists of the phylum euglenophyta but have significantly increased levels of movement speed, nutrient storage capability and resistance to alpha radiation.

I think that the foundation would say something like "SCP-118 is a protist of the phylum euglenophyta, of an unknown class".

When in a survivable salt water environment, specimens of SCP-118 will seek out materials including but not limited to iron, silver, copper, carbon, TNT, and uranium isotopes.

How are they going to find enough TNT and uranium-235 to make a bomb?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I not catch those? All SCP-XXX's should be gone now, thanks for pointing that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: some an\a mixup in the logs, along with a few scp-xxx's. A few test logs seemed silly, but that didn't stop my upvote.
